# A review on host-pathogen interactions: classification and prediction

### Affiliations

# A review on host-pathogen interactions: classification and prediction

### Authors

### Affiliations

## Abstract

The research on host-pathogen interactions is an ever-emerging and evolving field. Every other day a new pathogen gets discovered, along with comes the challenge of its prevention and cure. As the intelligent human always vies for prevention, which is better than cure, understanding the mechanisms of host-pathogen interactions gets prior importance. There are many mechanisms involved from the pathogen as well as the host sides while an interaction happens. It is a vis-a-vis fight of the counter genes and proteins from both sides. Who wins depends on whether a host gets an infection or not. Moreover, a higher level of complexity arises when the pathogens evolve and become resistant to a host's defense mechanisms. Such pathogens pose serious challenges for treatment. The entire human population is in danger of such long-lasting persistent infections. Some of these infections even increase the rate of mortality. Hence there is an immediate emergency to understand how the pathogens interact with their host for successful invasion. It may lead to discovery of appropriate preventive measures, and the development of rational therapeutic measures and medication against such infections and diseases. This review, a state-of-the-art updated scenario of host-pathogen interaction research, has been done by keeping in mind this urgency. It covers the biological and computational aspects of host-pathogen interactions, classification of the methods by which the pathogens interact with their hosts, different machine learning techniques for prediction of host-pathogen interactions, and future scopes of this research field.

**Keywords:**
Effector proteins; Host–pathogen interactions; In silico prediction; Machine learning; Pathogen informatics; Secretion systems.

Review

Predicting host-pathogen interactions with machine learning algorithms: A scoping review
================================================================================================

Author links open overlay panel Rasool Sahragard a, Masoud Arabfard b, Ali Najafi a

Show more

Outline

Add to Mendeley

Share

Cite

[https://doi.org/10.1016/j.meegid.2025.105751](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.meegid.2025.105751 "Persistent link using digital object identifier")[Get rights and content](https://s100.copyright.com/AppDispatchServlet?publisherName=ELS&contentID=S1567134825000401&orderBeanReset=true)

Under a Creative Commons [license](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/)

Open access

Highlights
----------

*   •This review evaluates studies on machine learning for HPI prediction, categorizing by host, pathogen, algorithms, and metrics. 
*   •Findings reveal that tree-based algorithms like Random Forest and Gradient Boosting in HPI prediction, while deep learning needs more labeled data. 
*   •The review identifies gaps in dataset standardization and model interpretability, stressing the need for better quality and transparency in AI. 

Abstract
--------

### Background

Diseases caused by pathogenic microorganisms pose a persistent global health challenge. Pathogens exploit host mechanisms through intricate molecular interactions. Understanding these host-pathogen interactions (HPIs), particularly protein-protein interactions (PPIs), is crucial for developing therapeutic strategies. While experimental approaches are essential, they are often labor-intensive and costly. Researchers have been able to predict HPIs more efficiently due to recent advances in artificial intelligence and machine learning. However, existing reviews lack a systematic evaluation of different machine learning methodologies and their effectiveness.

### Methods

This scoping review critically examines recent studies on machine learning-based Host-Pathogen Interaction (HPI) prediction, categorizing them by host and pathogen types, machine learning algorithms, and key evaluation metrics. The methodology is based on the study beginning with a preliminary search in reputable using key phrases related to host-pathogen interactions from 2019 to 2024. This process yielded 46 relevant articles, from which 30 were selected for review after evaluating titles and abstracts.

### Results

Our findings indicate that tree-based algorithms, particularly Random Forest and Gradient Boosting, are the most prevalent in Host-Pathogen Interaction (HPI) prediction. The filter articles were categorized by host and pathogen type and further subdivided into four subcategories based on the prediction type and machine learning algorithms: classic, tree-based, vector-based, and neural network algorithms. Convolutional and recurrent neural networks are among the deep learning models that demonstrate promising accuracy, but they require a lot of labeled data for effective training. Additionally, the analysis uncovers significant gaps in dataset standardization and model interpretability, which pose challenges to the broader applicability of these predictive models.

### Conclusion

In this review, we emphasize the potential of machine learning in HPI prediction and highlight the important challenges that must be addressed to improve predictive accuracy. Unlike previous reviews, our study systematically compares different computational approaches, offering a roadmap for future research. The findings emphasize the importance of dataset quality, feature selection, and model transparency in advancing AI-driven pathogen research.

*   Previous article in issue
*   [Next article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000267)

Keywords
--------

Host-pathogen interactions

Protein-protein interaction

Prediction algorithms

Machine learning algorithms

Bioinformatics

1. Introduction
---------------

The factors contributing to the disease are highly diverse regarding biochemical structure and genetic makeup. Due to minor changes in dynamic ecological balances, these factors stem from various sources and only occasionally surpass their threshold. Climatic changes and deviations from ecological equilibrium, along with other yet undiscovered dimensions, lead to the emergence of new infectious diseases ([Lahlali et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0065)). These instances render widespread outbreaks more probable. In order to combat and prevent future pandemics, researchers are striving to enhance their understanding of infectious diseases, particularly the interaction between pathogens (pathogenic microorganisms) and hosts ([Yakimovich, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0180)). Host-pathogen interaction (HPI) is a term used to describe how pathogens, such as viruses, bacteria, prion, fungus, and viroid, interact with their hosts. Pathogens are able to adapt to changes and find different strategies to survive and infect the host ([Poluri et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0100)). These infectious agents are responsible for causing diseases when the host's immune system is unable to mount an effective defense. Investigating host-pathogen interactions provides insights into critical questions regarding pathogen functionality, mechanisms of entry into the host through biological barriers, and their ability to persist in hosts that may be undergoing treatment or vaccination against them ([Syed et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0140)). The dynamics of host and pathogen interactions can be analyzed at various levels: population level (such as viral infections within human populations), organismal level (where pathogens invade individual hosts), and molecular level (including the binding of pathogen proteins to receptors on human cells) ([Sen et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0115)). This field began with scattered research on a pathogen and its interaction with the host. The initial investigations into host-pathogen interactions considered environmental factors such as light, temperature, season, and the population of pathogens- hosts ([Syed et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0140)). Later on, organisms such as _C. elegans_ and _D. melanogaster_ were identified as model organisms for studying pathogen behavior in other complex hosts (such as humans) due to their simple body plan, well-known genome structure, and short life cycle. Specific proteins and protein clusters were gradually identified to participate in host-pathogen interactions ([Tecle and Troemel, 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0155); [Henry et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0045)). Furthermore, with recent advancements in imaging techniques and molecular biology, specific classifications for the mechanisms of host-pathogen interactions have emerged ([Gómez Borrego and Torrent Burgas, 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0040)). Researchers in this field are attempting to use new techniques to examine and identify interactions between a pathogen and its host. Pathogens infiltrate host cells to replicate and spread, engaging in a series of protein-protein interactions (PPIs) with the host. These interactions are typically termed host-pathogen interactions ([Yakimovich, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0180)).

As an example of host-pathogen interactions, the mechanism of SARS-CoV-2 entry into human cells can be highlighted. The virus utilizes its spike (S) protein to bind to the ACE2 receptor on human cells, including those in the lungs, heart, and intestines. This binding occurs via the receptor-binding domain (RBD) within the S1 subunit, facilitating viral entry. Subsequently, host proteases, such as TMPRSS2, cleave the spike protein at specific sites, activating the S2 subunit, which promotes membrane fusion and enables the release of the viral RNA genome into the cytoplasm of the host cell ([Steiner et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0135)). Mutations such as N501Y enhance RBD binding affinity, increasing viral transmissibility ([Sarkar and Madabhavi, 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0105)). Furthermore, viral binding to ACE2 reduces receptor levels, disrupting angiotensin II degradation and exacerbating inflammatory responses such as cytokine storms ([Bar-On et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0010)). These molecular mechanisms underscore the complexity of host-pathogen interactions and highlight the significance of investigating these interactions for developing effective therapeutic strategies.

Understanding the biological mechanisms of entry and proliferation of pathogens may contribute to developing broad strategies for combating diseases. However, it should be noted that identifying host-pathogen interactions at the mechanistic level is often a laborious, experimental, and costly endeavor. Moreover, due to the adaptability of pathogens, especially viruses, deciphering host-pathogen interaction mechanisms is akin to pursuing a moving target ([Lahlali et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0065)). One of the tools that has given researchers hope in the timely identification of host-pathogen interactions is machine learning algorithms and artificial intelligence. While empirical validation and evaluation remain imperative, computational techniques in the field of artificial intelligence may significantly reduce the number of experiments required to identify host-pathogen interactions through prediction ([Yang et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0190)). The field of host-pathogen interactions predicts an important role in designing rational therapeutic interventions, such as drugs. It's possible for experimental methods to be time-consuming, cumbersome, and costly. Testing every possibility can take a significant amount of time. Machine learning can assist prediction methods in overcoming such challenges. They can initially be used to predict hypothetical data that fulfills specific conditions. Then, the predicted set can be empirically validated, requiring much less time and resources ([Kshirsagar et al., 2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0060)).

2. Research background
----------------------

### 2.1. Predicting host-pathogen interactions

The interactions between hosts and pathogens are determinant factors in the dynamics of infectious diseases. These interactions are mainly driven by protein-protein interactions (PPIs), which govern each phase of disease progression, starting from the initial invasion of the pathogen to its eventual establishment within the host ([Ul Qamar et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0165)). Proteins are large macromolecules made up of chains of amino acids. Protein-protein interactions (PPIs) are essential for nearly all cellular biological and biochemical processes. The majority of proteins carry out their functions through interactions with other proteins. These functions encompass nutrient transport, enzymatic reactions, immune responses, cell-to-cell communication, and tissue regeneration. Therefore, precise PPI identification is essential for understanding cellular physiology ([Soleymani et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0130)). In other words, predicting host-pathogen interactions involves predicting protein-protein interactions between the host and the pathogen based on recognizing protein sequences and infectious phenotypes (signs and symptoms) ([Liu-Wei et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0075)).

Moreover, monitoring and analyzing protein-protein interactions (PPIs) can yield valuable candidates for diagnostic and therapeutic applications in medicine, thereby aiding in the development of new drugs ([Trepte et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0160)). Therefore, the challenge of predicting PPIs is a fundamental research topic in systems biology and has thus garnered increased attention in recent years.

Various computational models have been proposed to overcome these drawbacks so that protein-protein interaction pairs can be systematically identified ([Hu et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0050)). In other words, the most extensive method by which a pathogen interacts with a host is Protein-Protein Interaction (PPI). In order to attack the host, pathogen proteins interact with host proteins. The host and its environment can be affected by pathogen proteins in different ways. They can directly interact with host protein(s), affecting subsequent reaction cascades and impairing the host's natural function(s). They can even jeopardize the host's immune defense by misleading and weakening it. They may exploit elements of a collapsing anaerobic environment in the damaged host from immune system attacks. Therefore, predicting potential PPIs between pathogens and their host(s) is of utmost importance ([Durmuş Tekir et al., 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0035)). Various computational exist to predict whether a host protein can interact with a pathogenic protein or vice versa. Current methods for predicting PPIs are generally classified into two categories: laboratory-based and computational ([Murmu et al., 2025](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0090)). Laboratory based methods have their own set of limitations. For instance, in experiments the first issue related to PPIs is usually the time-consuming and effort-intensive nature of these controls, which do not always lead to effective detection of the protein-protein interactions (PPI). Second, the PPI data obtained from these methods are in general incomplete imposed by the fact that assays have their limitations. Lastly, sufficient evidence for high rates of false positives and the like are among predictive results ([Yin et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0195)).

One of these approaches is the prediction based on physicochemical information. This information includes molecular weight, electric charge, solubility, isoelectric point, etc. The physicochemical information-based approach for predicting host-pathogen protein interactions is widely used due to its simplicity. However, this method has weaknesses because the predicted interactions based on this approach may not be significantly reliable due to neglecting other biological features. Therefore, there is a need for more information to increase prediction accuracy ([Tahir et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0145)). Another predictive method is structure-based prediction. When two proteins exhibit structural similarities to a known interacting protein pair, it can be inferred that these proteins may interact in a comparable manner. Another approach is motif-based prediction, which predicts the probability of interaction between two proteins based on the presence of specific motifs in their structure or sequence ([Palhamkhani et al., 2025](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0095)). Motifs are small parts of protein structures or sequences that have specific functions; for example, specific motifs may be responsible for binding to other proteins. By identifying and recognizing similar motifs in proteins, it is possible to predict whether these proteins may interact with each other or not ([Sen et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0115)).

### 2.2. Machine learning algorithms

The field of Artificial Intelligence (AI) emerged from efforts to create frameworks for machines with “intelligent features.” Machine Learning (ML), a subset of AI, has greatly contributed to this development by providing practical tools. Typically, a machine learning model is trained on a dataset containing features and variables that influence the target problem, using either supervised or unsupervised methods. Supervised learning involves independent variables (X) and dependent variables (Y), where Y represents a known target feature. These supervised learning techniques are particularly effective for automating the analysis of biological data, where defining precise rules for relevant events can be challenging. ([Yakimovich, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0180)).

In recent years, the development of machine learning algorithms has led to the emergence of new methodologies. Alongside classical machine learning algorithms, deep learning algorithms can be considered as a sophisticated evolution of machine learning algorithms. Deep learning has become highly prominent today, and the reason for this can be attributed to achieving results that seemed impossible before ([Chen et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0025)).

In recent years, machine learning and deep learning have gained significant attention as subsets of artificial intelligence. Machine learning enables algorithms to be autonomously adapted and enhanced based on incoming data, generating desired outputs without the need for human intervention. Deep learning, which is a subset of machine learning, employs multiple algorithms that each provide a different interpretation of the input data. This hierarchical structure is referred to as artificial neural networks, which endeavor to emulate the functionality of neural networks found in the human brain ([Mahapatra and Sahu, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0080)). Machine learning techniques were only occasionally utilized in the analysis of interactions between pathogens and their hosts in the field of biology before artificial intelligence was utilized. The use of machine learning and artificial intelligence techniques for analyzing host-pathogen interactions has become increasingly important due to the emergence of new diseases. One specific application of machine learning algorithms is the investigation of pathogens and protein interaction sequences to predict host-pathogen interactions, which has garnered significant interest among researchers ([Kaundal et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0055)).

The field of PPI prediction has been well-developed using machine learning models due to the availability of a vast amount of protein data generated by advanced technologies in recent years. Some researchers, such as Tang et al. provide a comprehensive review of machine learning-based prediction methods. The machine learning models used in these methods and the details of protein data representation in their research are specified. The discussion of the development process of machine learning-based methods has been raised to understand the potential advancements in predicting PPIs ([Tang et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0150)).

3. Research procedure
---------------------

In this research, considering the importance of utilizing new approaches in predicting host-pathogen interactions, efforts have been made to examine some recent studies in the field of host-pathogen interaction prediction using machine learning algorithms. To this end, the reviewed research has been analyzed and investigated in terms of the data sets used, the type of host and pathogen, the type of algorithm employed for prediction, independent variables for prediction, model evaluation indices, and how research results are presented. This research aims to categorize recent studies in the field of host-pathogen interaction to serve as a suitable guide for researchers. For this purpose, A systematic and step-by-step process was employed to collect articles related to the topic of “Prediction of Host”. The study identification and selection process has been illustrated in [Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#f0005), following the PRISMA framework.

Pathogen Interactions Using Machine Learning Algorithms.“ Initially, a preliminary search for articles related to the topic was conducted using reputable scientific databases such as PubMed, IEEE Xplore, and Google Scholar. In this stage, key phrases such as “Host-Pathogen Interactions, Host-Microbe Interaction Prediction, Host-viruses interaction prediction, protein-protein interaction prediction, Machine Learning Algorithms, Computational Biology, Pathogen Identification, Bioinformatics, Disease Prediction” were searched within the timeframe of 2019 to 2024. To ensure the relevance and quality of the selected studies, specific filters were applied during the literature search. The inclusion criteria were:
*   (1)studies published between 2019 and 2024, 
*   (2)research articles and review papers related to host-pathogen interaction prediction using machine learning, 
*   (3)studies that provided performance metrics for model evaluation, and. 
*   (4)articles available in reputable databases such as PubMed, IEEE Xplore, and Google Scholar. 

The exclusion criteria included:
*   (1)non-English articles, 
*   (2)studies without sufficient methodological details, and. 
*   (3)preprints or unpublished works without peer review.

# A deadly dance: the choreography of host–pathogen interactions, as revealed by single-cell technologies

[*Nature Communications*](/ncomms)
**volume 9**, Article number: 4638 (2018)
[Cite this article](#citeas)

8142 Accesses

43 Citations

6 Altmetric

[Metrics details](/articles/s41467-018-06214-0/metrics)

### Subjects

## Abstract

Pathogens have numerous mechanisms by which they replicate within a host, who in turn responds by developing innate and adaptive immune countermeasures to limit disease. The advent of high-content single-cell technologies has facilitated a greater understanding of the properties of host cells harboring infection, the host’s pathogen-specific immune responses, and the mechanisms pathogens have evolved to escape host control. Here we review these advances and argue for greater inclusion of higher resolution single-cell technologies into approaches for defining immune evasion mechanisms by pathogens.

## Introduction

The relationship between pathogens and their hosts is complex and has been brought about by continuous co-evolution. A network of host cells and molecules attempts to survey, detect, and eliminate pathogens. In turn, pathogens have evolved elegant and varied approaches for evading host immune responses. By examining host–pathogen interactions, we aim to better understand the fundamental mechanisms involved in infection and immunity, and to provide a foundation for the rational design of new prophylactic or treatment strategies. With the recent emergence of sensitive single-cell technology platforms, host–pathogen interactions can be studied with a resolution and depth not previously possible (Box [1](/articles/s41467-018-06214-0#Sec2) and Fig. [1](/articles/s41467-018-06214-0#Fig1)).

![figure 1](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41467-018-06214-0/MediaObjects/41467_2018_6214_Fig1_HTML.png)

Single cell approaches provide finer resolution and more accurate analysis of host–pathogen interactions than bulk analysis. **a** Populations of cells can be defined by shared or distinct phenotypes (e.g., naive vs. memory (gated)). These populations of cells may differ in frequency of infection, and individual cells may differ in the number of pathogen transcripts expressed. These features of host–pathogen interactions can be ascertained with single-cell approaches. **b** Assay of all cells in bulk provides an inaccurate estimate of pathogen burden: there is no information about the frequency of infection and reports an average number of pathogen transcripts per cell (which does not reflect the actual number of transcripts in any of the cells assayed). **c** Sorting of cell populations (e.g., by fluorescence-activated cell sorting) can better resolve relative differences in pathogen burden between cell phenotypes (e.g., central and effector memory, CM and EM, respectively), but remains misleading in terms of infection frequency and number of transcripts. **d** Single-cell analyses (e.g., cell sorting of one cell per sample well) reveals differential infection frequencies and pathogen burden per cell between CM and EM cell populations. In this example, infected cell frequency in CM exceeds that of EM (50% vs. 25%), but infected EM cells harbor a larger per cell viral transcript burden (12,500 vRNA copies vs. 2600)

The choreography between pathogen, target host cells, and immune surveillance dictates the course of disease, and is likely to define transitions between acute, chronic, and latent infection, as well as transmission. Studying these interactions is complicated by changes to pathogen replication, persistence and resistance, throughout its life cycle. Many pathogens, such as malaria[1](/articles/s41467-018-06214-0#ref-CR1 "Baum, J., Gilberger, T. W., Frischknecht, F. & Meissner, M. Host-cell invasion by malaria parasites: insights from Plasmodium and Toxoplasma. Trends Parasitol. 24, 557–563 (2008)."), alter their host cell tropism during the course of disease, and others such as HIV[2](/articles/s41467-018-06214-0#ref-CR2 "Siliciano, R. F. & Greene, W. C. HIV latency. Cold Spring Harb. Perspect. Med. 1, a007096 (2011).") and herpesviruses[3](/articles/s41467-018-06214-0#ref-CR3 "Lieberman, P. M. Epigenetics and genetics of viral latency. Cell Host Microbe 19, 619–628 (2016).") adopt latent infection states invisible to immune responses. Thus, preventing chronic and latent infections, immune evasion, and transmission requires an understanding of host–pathogen interactions at a finite level.

Single-cell technologies relevant to the study of host–pathogen interactions are listed in Table [1](/articles/s41467-018-06214-0#Tab1) and Fig. [2](/articles/s41467-018-06214-0#Fig2) and a general overview of these methods is provided in Box [2](/articles/s41467-018-06214-0#Sec3). Using these platforms, significant advances have been made in understanding both successful and ineffective pathogen-specific immune responses, profiling pathogens, and understanding how host cell biology is affected by pathogen infection. For this, single-cell analyses have been invaluable. Here we review how the application of single-cell technologies has advanced our understanding of pathogen-specific immune responses, infected host cell profiles, and pathogen characteristics.

![figure 2](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41467-018-06214-0/MediaObjects/41467_2018_6214_Fig2_HTML.png)

Heat map comparing various single-cell technology platforms. Relative capabilities of the single-cell technologies listed at top (columns) are depicted for each feature (rows). Performance is ranked from low to high relative to the other technologies using the indicated color scheme

### Box 1 Advantages of single-cell analyses over bulk measurements

Single-cell measurements are defined as quantitation of one or more analytes (e.g., RNA, DNA, protein) associated with a single cell. Depending on the method, the assay may be performed on a cell residing within a tissue section or in suspension. Single cell-based approaches applied to the study of hosts and pathogens offer important advantages over bulk cell analysis (Fig. [1](/articles/s41467-018-06214-0#Fig1)). The latter often relies on a mix of infected and uninfected cells and responding and nonresponding cells. Thus, signal from uninfected or non-specific cells dilutes information about host–pathogen interactions. In some cases, critical proteins associated with the cells of interest may be diluted below the limit of detection, while background signal from uninvolved cells increases. This is a particularly impactful problem, because pathogens are typically small (with much lower protein, DNA, and RNA content than cells) and because infected cells are typically rare. Bulk analysis also averages information about protein or transcript expression, so that precise cell-by-cell phenotypes cannot be assessed. Knowledge of the specific phenotype of infected cells, or responding immune cells, may help reveal mechanisms of infection, immune evasion, or successful immunity. Perhaps most important is that bulk analysis provides no information about the coordinate expression of multiple molecules in individual cells. At a minimum, this occludes the ongoing communication occurring between pathogen and host, and amongst cells in the host immune system. Despite these limitations, comparisons of bulk to single-cell measurements should be performed during the initial evaluation of any single-cell technology, in order to demonstrate that there is no loss of assay sensitivity at the single-cell level. An approach to performing single-cell/bulk comparisons is demonstrated for highly multiplexed qPCR by Dominguez et al.[64](/articles/s41467-018-06214-0#ref-CR64 "Dominguez, M. H. et al. Highly multiplexed quantitation of gene expression on single cells. J. Immunol. Methods 
                           391, 133–145 (2013).").

### Box 2 Overview of single-cell technologies and platforms

Single-cell technologies are now available for a wide array of settings, including tissue and cell suspensions, in vitro and in vivo studies, and mRNA and protein analysis. In this article, we review imaging platforms (like histocytometry and two-photon imaging), high parameter cytometry-based technologies (like fluorescence flow cytometry and mass cytometry), in situ hybridization approaches (like RNAscope and PrimeFlow), and single-cell molecular platforms (like highly multiplexed single cell qPCR and RNA-seq). Beyond these platforms, there are many new extraordinarily sensitive and exciting technologies that are being applied to single-cell analyses, such as super-resolution imaging, in situ PCR analyses, introduction of multiple fluorescent reporters at key genetic sites in cells or animals. These will certainly inform host–pathogen (and host-cancer) interactions in the future, but have not been broadly applied as yet. Thus, those technologies are not covered here, but we hope that the successes reviewed here will spur the application of such technologies to this subject. In addition, we focus primarily on studies employing intact pathogens in human or animal models of infection, with less emphasis on studies involving pathogen components (e.g., nucleic acid or pathogen-associated molecular pattern compounds) and in vitro tissue culture models. Whole organism studies are more likely to capture the full range of complex interactions between host and pathogen.

## Pathogen-specific immune responses

Immune responses are inherently complex, involving many different functional types of cells, and often occur in highly organized tissue microenvironments. Technologies that visualise the three dimensional organizations of host responses to infection, and those that enable the isolation of cells for further analyses, have advanced our understanding of how pathogens influence host immune responses. Both approaches enable the simultaneous measurement of multiple parameters, revealing how immune responses are initiated and coordinated in the tissue microenvironment, and they facilitate in-depth characterization of transcripts or protein in both natural infection and vaccine settings.

### Histocytometry

Imaging technologies, such as histocytometry (see Table [1](/articles/s41467-018-06214-0#Tab1)), address fundamental questions in immunology, such as how human T-cells provide helper signals for antibody-secreting B-cells. To date, a major limitation to the study of this process has been the lack of readily available draining lymph nodes, which is the tissue where these interactions are thought to occur. This is primarily because these tissues are not routinely sampled after pathogen exposure, and the often invasive nature required to obtain samples; indeed, the gut- and lung-associated lymph nodes require surgery for sampling.

Understanding the phenotype, function, and localization of CD8+ T-cells within lymph nodes has important implications for the eradication of chronic viral infections, even in treated individuals. During HIV infections, lymph nodes, particularly their germinal centers and follicles, are an active site of viral replication and re-activation from latency, and CD8+ T-cells play an important role in killing HIV-infected cells. In 2017, Petrovas et al. used histocytometry to study CD8+ T-cells in the lymph nodes of HIV-infected individuals[4](/articles/s41467-018-06214-0#ref-CR4 "Petrovas, C. et al. Follicular CD8 T cells accumulate in HIV infection and can kill infected cells in vitro via bispecific antibodies. Sci. Transl. Med. 9, eaag2285 (2017)."). The authors showed that CXCR5hi CD8+ T-cells accumulate in germinal centers, demarcated by co-expression of CD20 and Ki67. Detailed characterization of the heterogeneity in the differentiation and checkpoint markers within these cells showed that they are capable of potent cytolytic function. Furthermore, these can be directed to kill HIV-infected cells using bi-specific antibodies that bind HIV proteins on the cell surface and CD3 on the surface of the follicular CD8+ T-cell. In this particular example, histocytometry facilitated the mapping of these complex cellular phenotypes to lymph node germinal centers at single-cell resolution.

Recently, histocytometry has been used to show that tonsil tissues obtained from children contain an organized microanatomy that is typical of secondary, draining lymph nodes[5](/articles/s41467-018-06214-0#ref-CR5 "Amodio, D. et al. Quantitative multiplexed imaging analysis reveals a strong association between immunogen-specific B cell responses and tonsillar germinal center immune dynamics in children after influenza vaccination. J. Immunol. 200, 538–550 (2018)."). Of note, the tonsils studied contained clear germinal center and follicle structures. Within these structures, follicular helper T-cells (TFH) have been shown to provide key signals for the proliferation, differentiation, somatic hypermutation, and isotype-switching of B-cells. Thus, tonsil tissue was confirmed as a model for draining lymph nodes. Amodio et al. next studied responses to seasonal influenza vaccine within the tonsil tissue microenvironment[5](/articles/s41467-018-06214-0#ref-CR5 "Amodio, D. et al. Quantitative multiplexed imaging analysis reveals a strong association between immunogen-specific B cell responses and tonsillar germinal center immune dynamics in children after influenza vaccination. J. Immunol. 200, 538–550 (2018)."), finding higher levels of TFH cells in vaccinated compared to unvaccinated children. Notably, regulatory cells that suppress TFH were localized to extrafollicular areas of the tonsil, and these cells were diminished in vaccinated children. Cell subsets were profiled for a number of other markers in the study, providing multidimensional information about the organization and activity of the helper T-cell response after vaccination. In sum, this study demonstrated that a readily accessible tissue could provide an important model for how immune responses are staged in lymph nodes.

### In vivo imaging

Although powerful, a limitation of histocytometry is that it only provides a snapshot of an immune response at a single, static time point. Immune responses are dynamic as cells are recruited, engage the pathogen or infected cells, and then return to unactivated or memory states. The study of these dynamic aspects requires in vivo imaging technologies such as two-photon imaging (see Table [1](/articles/s41467-018-06214-0#Tab1)). The remarkable resolution of two-photon imaging enables location information to be provided at both the broader tissue level and within tens of microns around single cells.

In 2013, two-photon intravital imaging revealed dense clusters of neutrophils that rearrange local collagen networks in tissues, following infection with *Pseudomonas aeruginosa*, which leads to better access to the pathogen by host immune cells[6](/articles/s41467-018-06214-0#ref-CR6 "Lammermann, T. et al. Neutrophil swarms require LTB4 and integrins at sites of cell death in vivo. Nature 498, 371–375 (2013)."). Upon infection of a mouse with *P. aeruginosa*, neutrophils are recruited by the lipid leukotriene B4, which is released by dying host cells at the site of infection. In this way, leukotriene B4 amplifies “danger” signals, increasing the distance from which neutrophils can be recruited. Furthermore, two-photon intravital imaging identified an important role for the integrin receptor in maintaining these neutrophil clusters, and the rearrangement of collagen networks. Prior to this, integrin receptors had not been associated with this cell-to-cell contact function in host–defense; they were thought to be dispensable for neutrophil migration.

The ability to contextualize single-cell data represents an important virtue of imaging technologies—and underscores limitations of studying the immune system in cells removed from these contexts. In an example from 2012, Muller et al. demonstrated that CD4+ T-cells specific for *Leishmania major* had direct contact with only a minority of infected cells[7](/articles/s41467-018-06214-0#ref-CR7 "Muller, A. J. et al. CD4+ T cells rely on a cytokine gradient to control intracellular pathogens beyond sites of antigen presentation. Immunity 
                           37, 147–157 (2012)."). This was surprising as it showed the immune system honing in on specific pathogen-infected cells; however, significantly, the authors found that IFNγ, secreted as a consequence of this relatively rare immune:infected cell interaction, was distributed across a gradient up to 80 μm away from the immunological synapse. This remarkable finding suggests the promotion of a bystander effect, whereby intracellular defense mechanisms are triggered in neighboring cells despite minimal initial immune:infected cell interaction.

Single-cell imaging also identifies changes in immune cell behavior after exposure to pathogens. For persistent pathogens, two-photon imaging identified changes that lead to anergized T-cells. In a lymphocytic choriomeningitis virus (LCMV) model of chronic infection, two-photon imaging showed that the motility of LCMV-specific CD4+ and CD8+ T-cells is reduced within the lymph node of chronically infected animals, and that this “motility paralysis” is facilitated by the binding of the exhaustion molecule, PD1, to its ligand, PDL1[8](/articles/s41467-018-06214-0#ref-CR8 "Zinselmeyer, B. H. et al. PD-1 promotes immune exhaustion by inducing antiviral T cell motility paralysis. J. Exp. Med. 210, 757–774 (2013)."). Similarly, in *L. major*, within minutes after exposure intradermal dendritic cells became immobilized[9](/articles/s41467-018-06214-0#ref-CR9 "Ng, L. G. et al. Migratory dermal dendritic cells act as rapid sensors of protozoan parasites. PLoS Pathog. 4, e1000222 (2008)."). However, unlike the exhausted T-cells in the LCMV model, immobilized dendritic cells were thought to still participate in immune responses because they are able to phagocytose *L. major* efficiently. However, subsequent studies showed that the immune response to *L. major* is limited in a variety of ways, across different parts of the infected tissue. Despite phagocytic activity of immobilized dendritic cells, in some tissue regions, dendritic cells carrying *L. major* accumulated and left little access for T-cells[10](/articles/s41467-018-06214-0#ref-CR10 "Filipe-Santos, O. et al. A dynamic map of antigen recognition by CD4 T cells at the site of Leishmania major infection. Cell Host Microbe 
                           6, 23–33 (2009)."). In other tissue regions, CD4+ T-cells interact with infected phagocytes but these interactions are often short-lived, suggesting suboptimal or inefficient immune responses. A similar immune arrest phenomenon may occur during *Mycobacteria* infection, where T-cells within the granuloma only weakly produce cytokines in vivo, despite exhibiting robust cytokine production in vitro[11](/articles/s41467-018-06214-0#ref-CR11 "Egen, J. G. et al. Intravital imaging reveals limited antigen presentation and T cell effector function in mycobacterial granulomas. Immunity 
                           34, 807–819 (2011).").

Taken together, these studies show that pathogens can “dance around” immune responses in complex ways, and that high parameter tissue imaging of single cells is well suited to define the steps in this dance.

### High parameter, high throughput single-cell technology

Immune responses to pathogens involve a variety of cells; often, specific subsets of cells within both innate and adaptive arms of immunity are invoked. Specific functions of responding immune cells may be diverse, for example multiple cytokines can be expressed simultaneously or in specific combinations; such finite characterization can only be done using high parameter single-cell technologies.

High parameter, high throughput technologies such as flow cytometry, qPCR, and RNA-seq (see Table [1](/articles/s41467-018-06214-0#Tab1)) enable broad investigation and characterization of immune lineages, and the functions of all cells involved in a response. Such technologies revealed how host-responding cells change over the course of an immune response, differ amongst individuals, and even change with circadian cycles[12](/articles/s41467-018-06214-0#ref-CR12 "Druzd, D. et al. Lymphocyte circadian clocks control lymph node trafficking and adaptive immune responses. Immunity 
                           46, 120–132 (2017).").

### Flow cytometry

Both fluorescence-activated cell sorting (FACS) and mass spectrometry-based cytometry (CyTOF) are used to interrogate immune responses[13](/articles/s41467-018-06214-0#ref-CR13 "Chattopadhyay, P. K., Gierahn, T. M., Roederer, M. & Love, J. C. Single-cell technologies for monitoring immune systems. Nat. Immunol. 15, 128–135 (2014)."). There are advantages to either technique. FACS allows high-throughput analysis, compatibility with a large body of reagents, and the ability to isolate individual or large numbers of viable cells for further profiling. CyTOF allows ease of multiparameter antibody panel development, and a higher level of multiplexing.

High parameter cytometry approaches have been used to assess multiple aspects of cell phenotype and function, including differentiation state, proliferation potential, trafficking, cytotoxic capacity, and cytokine secretion. Notably, many of these traits are co-expressed in combinations that can define, very precisely, cellular states[13](/articles/s41467-018-06214-0#ref-CR13 "Chattopadhyay, P. K., Gierahn, T. M., Roederer, M. & Love, J. C. Single-cell technologies for monitoring immune systems. Nat. Immunol. 15, 128–135 (2014)."). Over the past decade, a number of platforms based on detection of fluorescence, elemental isotopic, or molecular signals have emerged for high parameter analysis. These powerful technologies have been used recently for improved characterization of human immune responses and their variation. In 2014, individuals were studied before and after vaccination for seasonal influenza using polychromatic flow cytometry[14](/articles/s41467-018-06214-0#ref-CR14 "Tsang, J. S. et al. Global analyses of human immune variation reveal baseline predictors of postvaccination responses. Cell 
                           157, 499–513 (2014)."). There was remarkable inter-individual variation in cell frequencies before vaccination, with evidence of remodeling of the peripheral immune compartment following vaccination, including the upregulation of hundreds of transcripts. Most importantly, baseline pre-vaccination immune cell populations could predict antibody titers post-vaccination, suggesting that immunophenotyping of healthy individuals before vaccination could be used to predict vaccine efficacy. Indeed, this concept was suggested in 2015 in a study of immune parameters in twins using both fluorescence flow and mass cytometry[15](/articles/s41467-018-06214-0#ref-CR15 "Brodin, P. et al. Variation in the human immune system is largely driven by non-heritable influences. Cell 
                           160, 37–47 (2015)."); 58% of the measured immune traits were determined by non-heritable factors. This trend was similar to that seen for immune parameters following vaccination; antibody responses to seasonal influenza vaccination were driven by non-heritable influences, and more than half of immune parameters in twins discordant for cytomegalovirus (CMV) exposure differed. These results demonstrate how single-cell technologies can reveal intricate heterogeneity in immune responses to vaccination, and can help to understand the genetic basis and predictive power of finely defined subsets.

### Single-cell transcriptomics

Single-cell transcriptomics enables the quantification of mRNA from individual cells. Both qPCR and sequencing measure RNA from multiple single cells at the same time, which provides quantitative coordinated gene expression data. Multiplexed qPCR was an early approach to single-cell gene expression analysis and targets a specific set of genes, typically pre-defined by the user. However, as the economics and sensitivity of single-cell mRNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) improved, it has increasingly substituted qPCR, as it allows quantification of many more mRNAs and in an unbiased manner.

Indeed, an early approach for deep profiling of immune cells combined FACS with highly multiplexed single-cell qPCR[16](/articles/s41467-018-06214-0#ref-CR16 "Flatz, L. et al. Single-cell gene-expression profiling reveals qualitatively distinct CD8 T cells elicited by different gene-based vaccines. Proc. Natl Acad. Sci. USA 
                           108, 5724–5729 (2011)."). Single cells defined by expression of a dozen or more proteins were isolated, and then quantified for the expression of a minimum of 96 mRNA transcripts (see Table [1](/articles/s41467-018-06214-0#Tab1)). In 2011, this approach was used to identify mechanisms of protection by vaccines; specifically, the comparison of HIV-specific T-cells generated by three different vaccine regimens: (1) DNA vaccine prime/recombinant adenoviral vaccine boost, (2) recombinant adenoviral prime and boost, and (3) recombinant adenoviral prime/recombinant LCMV boost[16](/articles/s41467-018-06214-0#ref-CR16 "Flatz, L. et al. Single-cell gene-expression profiling reveals qualitatively distinct CD8 T cells elicited by different gene-based vaccines. Proc. Natl Acad. Sci. USA 
                           108, 5724–5729 (2011)."). When assayed by standard flow cytometry, vaccine-specific cells were indistinguishable across the three regimens in terms of frequency, phenotype, or functionality. However, 96-parameter mRNA analysis distinguished the vaccine regimens, and identified regimen-specific gene signatures. Notably, the regimen using a DNA prime elicited more central memory-like than effector memory-like T-cells.

The power of scRNA-seq was used to show that antigen-induced cells functionally adapt to local tissue environments to stop the spread of a pathogen throughout the organism based on finite profiling of genes specific to lung vs. liver T-cells[17](/articles/s41467-018-06214-0#ref-CR17 "Kadoki, M. et al. Organism-level analysis of vaccination reveals networks of protection across tissues. Cell 171, 398–413 (2017)."). In contrast, another approach showed how local reprogramming of cells can help spread infection with varicella zoster virus (VZV)[18](/articles/s41467-018-06214-0#ref-CR18 "Sen, N., Mukherjee, G. & Arvin, A. M. Single cell mass cytometry reveals remodeling of human T cell phenotypes by varicella zoster virus. Methods 90, 85–94 (2015)."). This is counterintuitive to how we understand the immune response, but evidence from tonsil tissues infected with VZV has shown that the virus can infect various types of T-cells, and that infection reprogrammes a variety of cells to express markers for skin homing, thus driving infected T-cells to propagate infection in the skin and likely aid virus transmission.

### High parameter single-cell imaging

Two recent technologies have adapted high parameter approaches to imaging platforms in remarkable ways. The first, known as Imaging Mass Cytometry (IMC), employs lanthanide-tagged antibodies, similar to suspension mass cytometry, but using laser ablation to collect cell staining signals; the resulting cloud of lanthanides at each location in the tissue is collected and transferred to a standard CyTOF instrument[19](/articles/s41467-018-06214-0#ref-CR19 "Giesen, C. et al. Highly multiplexed imaging of tumor tissues with subcellular resolution by mass cytometry. Nat. Methods 
                           11, 417–422 (2014)."). Although this approach has not yet been applied to profiling immune responses or pathogen-infected cells, it is likely possible, given a recent report describing combined analysis of mRNA and protein expression in tissue samples from breast cancer[20](/articles/s41467-018-06214-0#ref-CR20 "Schulz, D. et al. Simultaneous multiplexed imaging of mRNA and proteins with subcellular resolution in

enriched_points.expanded_content: Conducting a thorough literature review is essential to understand existing knowledge, identify gaps, and formulate research questions regarding host-pathogen interactions as outlined in recent studies.

Data analysis involves utilizing computational methods and machine learning algorithms to predict host-pathogen interactions, enhancing the understanding of protein-protein interactions crucial for therapeutic developments.

Experimental studies validate predictions made from computational models, employing controlled environments to examine host-pathogen dynamics and immune responses, providing empirical evidence for theoretical frameworks.
